input_row.title: Ensure that ‘Enable Infrastructure Encryption’ for Each Storage Account in Azure Storage is Set to ‘enabled’
input_row.description: Enabling encryption at the hardware level on top of the default software encryption for Storage Accounts accessing Azure storage solutions.
input_row.rationale: Azure Storage automatically encrypts all data in a storage account at the network level using 256-bit AES encryption, which is one of the strongest, FIPS 140-2-compliant block ciphers available. Customers who require higher levels of assurance that their data is secure can also enable 256-bit AES encryption at the Azure Storage infrastructure level for double encryption. Double encryption of Azure Storage data protects against a scenario where one of the encryption algorithms or keys may be compromised. Similarly, data is encrypted even before network transmission and in all backups. In this scenario, the additional layer of encryption continues to protect your data. For the most secure implementation of key based encryption, it is recommended to use a Customer Managed asymmetric RSA 2048 Key in Azure Key Vault. Impact: The read and write speeds to the storage will be impacted if both default encryption and Infrastructure Encryption are checked, as a secondary form of encryption requires more resource overhead for the cryptography of information. This performance impact should be considered in an analysis for justifying use of the feature in your environment. Customer-managed keys are recommended for the most secure implementation, leading to overhead of key management. The key will also need to be backed up in a secure location, as loss of the key will mean loss of the information in the storage.
input_row.audit: Audit from Azure Portal 1. From Azure Portal select the portal menu in the top left. 2. Select Storage Accounts. 3. Click on each storage account within each resource group you wish to audit. 4. In the overview, under Security, ensure Infrastructure encryption is set to Enabled.  Audit from Azure CLI az storage blob show \ --account-name <storage-account> \ --container-name <container> \ --name <blob> \ --query "properties.serverEncrypted" Audit from PowerShell $account = Get-AzStorageAccount -ResourceGroupName <resource-group> ` -Name <storage-account> $blob = Get-AzStorageBlob -Context $account.Context ` -Container <container> ` -Blob <blob> $blob.ICloudBlob.Properties.IsServerEncrypted Audit from Azure Policy If referencing a digital copy of this Benchmark, clicking a Policy ID will open a link to the associated Policy definition in Azure. If referencing a printed copy, you can search Policy IDs from this URL: https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Policy/PolicyMenuBlade/~/Definitions • Policy ID: 4733ea7b-a883-42fe-8cac-97454c2a9e4a - Name: 'Storage accounts should have infrastructure encryption'
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal 1. During Storage Account creation, in the Encryption tab, check the box next to Enable infrastructure encryption. Remediate from Azure CLI Replace the information within <> with appropriate values: az storage account create \ --name <storage-account> \ --resource-group <resource-group> \ --location <location> \ --sku Standard_RAGRS \ --kind StorageV2 \ --require-infrastructure-encryption Remediate from PowerShell Replace the information within <> with appropriate values: New-AzStorageAccount -ResourceGroupName <resource_group> ` -AccountName <storage-account> ` -Location <location> ` -SkuName "Standard_RAGRS" ` -Kind StorageV2 ` -RequireInfrastructureEncryption Enabling Infrastructure Encryption after Storage Account Creation If infrastructure encryption was not enabled on blob storage creation, there is no official way to enable it. Please see the additional information section. Default Value: By default, Infrastructure Encryption is disabled in blob creation. References: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/storage-blob-encryption- status 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-service- encryption 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/infrastructure- encryption-enable 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-data- protection#dp-4-enable-data-at-rest-encryption-by-default Additional Information: The default service side encryption for Azure Storage is enabled on every block blob, append blob, or page blob that was written to Azure Storage after October 20, 2017. Hardware encryption, however, cannot be enabled on a blob storage after its creation. There are ways to copy all data from a blob storage into another or download and reupload into another blob storage. This could result in data loss and is not recommended.
input_row.impact: The read and write speeds to the storage will be impacted if both default encryption and Infrastructure Encryption are checked, as a secondary form of encryption requires more resource overhead for the cryptography of information. This performance impact should be considered in an analysis for justifying use of the feature in your environment. Customer-managed keys are recommended for the most secure implementation, leading to overhead of key management. The key will also need to be backed up in a secure location, as loss of the key will mean loss of the information in the storage.
input_row.reference: 1. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/blobs/storage-blob-encryption- status 2. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-service- encryption 3. https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/infrastructure- encryption-enable 4. https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/security/benchmark/azure/mcsb-data- protection#dp-4-enable-data-at-rest-encryption-by-default Additional Information: The default service side encryption for Azure Storage is enabled on every block blob, append blob, or page blob that was written to Azure Storage after October 20, 2017. Hardware encryption, however, cannot be enabled on a blob storage after its creation. There are ways to copy all data from a blob storage into another or download and reupload into another blob storage. This could result in data loss and is not recommended.

first_opinion.response:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:** 
The audit can be fully verified using Azure Resource Manager APIs and Azure CLI. The control involves evaluating the encryption setting of Azure Storage accounts which can be queried through provided CLI commands and APIs specified in the audit section. Additionally, it directly mentions using Azure CLI and PowerShell for verification, indicating that API checks are possible.

**PROGRAM NAME:** azure_storage_account_infrastructure_encryption_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
The automated program will perform the following checks:
- Use Azure Resource Manager APIs or Azure CLI commands to list and check the configuration of Azure Storage accounts.
- Enumerate storage accounts across all subscriptions or specified resource groups to verify the infrastructure encryption setting.
  
**Specific steps include:**
1. **API/CLI Command to List Storage Accounts:**
   ```bash
   az storage account list --query "[].{Name:name, ResourceGroup:resourceGroup, Encryption:encryption}" --output table
   ```
   or using PowerShell:
   ```powershell
   Get-AzStorageAccount | Select-Object StorageAccountName, ResourceGroupName, EnableInfrastructureEncryption
   ```

2. **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
   - Look for the `enableInfrastructureEncryption` property in the storage account settings.
   - Ensure this property is set to `true`.

**Pass/Fail Criteria:**
- **Pass:** If the `enableInfrastructureEncryption` property equals `true` for all storage accounts checked.
- **Fail:** If any storage account has `enableInfrastructureEncryption` property set to `false`.

**Example API Check:**
- The command to check a specific storage account's encryption setting:
   ```bash
   az storage account show --name <storage-account> --resource-group <resource-group> --query "encryption"
   ```

This automated program will significantly enhance compliance oversight for the specified security control and optimize configuration auditing for Azure Storage accounts.

second_opinion.response:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The control can be effectively verified using Azure Resource Manager APIs, Azure CLI, and PowerShell cmdlets. These tools provide the necessary capabilities to query the encryption settings of Azure Storage accounts, making manual checks unnecessary for this control.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9
- The automation details are technically accurate. The use of Azure CLI and PowerShell commands to list and check the encryption settings of storage accounts is appropriate. The commands provided are correct and align with Azure's capabilities to query storage account properties.
- The program name follows the convention: `azure_storage_account_infrastructure_encryption_enabled`, which is clear and descriptive.
- One minor improvement could be to explicitly mention the API version used in the Azure CLI commands to ensure compatibility with future changes.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**
- Consider adding error handling and logging to the automation script to capture any issues during execution, such as API call failures or permission errors.
- It might be beneficial to include a step to verify that the Azure CLI or PowerShell session has the necessary permissions to access the storage account settings.
- Provide guidance on how to remediate non-compliant storage accounts, such as steps to enable infrastructure encryption if it is found to be disabled.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH
- The assessment is well-structured, and the use of Azure APIs and CLI for automation is appropriate and reliable. The technical details provided are accurate and align with Azure's current capabilities.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**
- Keep as-is, with a suggestion to incorporate the improvements mentioned above. The automated approach is suitable for this control, and the technical execution is sound. Adding error handling and remediation guidance would enhance the robustness and utility of the audit process.

final_decision.response:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using Azure Resource Manager APIs, Azure CLI, and PowerShell. The compliance control involves checking the encryption settings of Azure Storage accounts, which can be effectively verified through these tools. The properties required for this check are exposed through standard Azure resource APIs, making manual inspection unnecessary.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

--- IF AUTOMATED ---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
azure_storage_account_infrastructure_encryption_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Azure Resource/Service: Storage Accounts
- API/CLI Method: `az storage account show`, `Get-AzStorageAccount`
- Properties to Check: `properties.encryption.services.blob.enabled`, `properties.encryption.keySource`
- Pass Criteria: Infrastructure encryption is set to 'enabled'
- Fail Criteria: Infrastructure encryption is not set to 'enabled'

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
There were no disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported the automated approach. The decision was made based on the capabilities of Azure APIs to verify the encryption settings programmatically.
